coredata.dc:title: Dual-Modulation ratiometric fluorescence strategy for cobalt and topotecan detection using Red-Emissive carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Alaseem, Ali M.
Alhazzani, Khalid
Alanazi, Ahmed Z.
Alsanad, Saud M.
Alkhamees, Osama A.
Alasiri, Glowi
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: A new ratiometric fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective detection of cobalt ions (Co2+) and topotecan (TOP) is proposed. The dual-probe system consists of red-emissive nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dots (R-NS@CDs) and TOP. For Co2+ detection, the fluorescence of R-NS@CDs at 680 nm is enhanced upon addition of Co2+, while complexed TOP emission peak at 545 nm remains constant. This enables ratiometric detection of Co2+ over a range of 5.0–160.0 ng mL−1. The mechanism of R-NS@CDs fluorescence enhancement by Co2+ is elucidated using FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, zeta potential measurements, and TEM imaging. For the detection of TOP, a ratiometric probe system comprising R-NS@CDs and Co2+ was utilized. TOP forms a complex with Co2+ bounded to R-NS@CDs, quenching the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ fluorescence and simultaneously the native TOP fluorescence is enhanced. This allows ratiometric quantification of TOP from 1.0-90.0 ng mL−1. The method provides high selectivity and low detection limits of 1.51 ng mL−1 for Co2+ and 0.37 ng mL−1 for TOP. Practical applicability is demonstrated through selective detection of Co2+ in environmental water samples and TOP in real plasma samples. The built-in self-calibration enabled by dual analyte modulation of R-NS@CDs makes this a simple and powerful analytical approach.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Red emissive carbon dots
Cobalt ion
Topotecan
Dual-probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantum yield of the prepared R-NS@CDs
Analysis of TOP in real plasma samples (Pharmacokinetic study)
Determination of Co2+ in environmental waters
Analytical performances for Co2+ and TOP
Analysis of Co2+ in real water samples
Application to real plasma and pharmacokinetic study of TOP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their gratitude to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) for their generous support and funding. The grant provided (grant number IMSIU-RG23083) has been instrumental in the successful completion of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: particle size distribution), (B) XRD pattern, and (C) FT-IR spectrum of R-NS@CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) O1s, (D) N1s, and (E) S2p of the R-NS@CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation (λex = 300 nm/ 600 nm), and fluorescence emission (λem = 680 nm) spectra of the R-NS@CDs (B) Fluorescence spectra of the R-NS@CDs at different fluorescence excitation wavelengths (260 nm – 320 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-NS@CDs-complexed TOP system with different concentrations of Co2+. (B) Linear relationship of F680/F545 versus Co2+ concentration ranged from 5.0 to 160.0 ng mL−1. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system with different concentrations of TOP. (D) Linear relationship of F545/F680 versus TOP concentration ranged from 1.0 to 90.0 ng mL−1. All experiments were done in triplicate and error bars indicate SD of triplicate measurements and RSD% (0.43––1.91).

(A) Fluorescence spectra overlay of R-NS@CDs, R-NS@CDs-Co2+ and R-NS@CDs- Co2+ + TOP (20.0 ng mL−1), (B) FT-IR spectral overlay of R-NS@CDs and R-NS@CDs-Co2+, (C) Zeta potential of R-NS@CDs and R-NS@CDs-Co2+, (D) Plot of

F
versus log[Q], (E) TEM image of R-NS@CDs after addition of Co2+, (F) TEM image of R-NS@CDs-Co2+ after addition of TOP (20.0 ng mL−1).
Results for the determination of Co2+ in real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dual-Modulation ratiometric fluorescence strategy for cobalt and topotecan detection using Red-Emissive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, College of Medicine, Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh 5701, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, College of Medicine, Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh 5701, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new ratiometric fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective detection of cobalt ions (Co2+) and topotecan (TOP) is proposed. The dual-probe system consists of red-emissive nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dots (R-NS@CDs) and TOP. For Co2+ detection, the fluorescence of R-NS@CDs at 680 nm is enhanced upon addition of Co2+, while complexed TOP emission peak at 545 nm remains constant. This enables ratiometric detection of Co2+ over a range of 5.0–160.0 ng mL−1. The mechanism of R-NS@CDs fluorescence enhancement by Co2+ is elucidated using FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, zeta potential measurements, and TEM imaging. For the detection of TOP, a ratiometric probe system comprising R-NS@CDs and Co2+ was utilized. TOP forms a complex with Co2+ bounded to R-NS@CDs, quenching the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ fluorescence and simultaneously the native TOP fluorescence is enhanced. This allows ratiometric quantification of TOP from 1.0-90.0 ng mL−1. The method provides high selectivity and low detection limits of 1.51 ng mL−1 for Co2+ and 0.37 ng mL−1 for TOP. Practical applicability is demonstrated through selective detection of Co2+ in environmental water samples and TOP in real plasma samples. The built-in self-calibration enabled by dual analyte modulation of R-NS@CDs makes this a simple and powerful analytical approach.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Topotecan (TOP), a chemotherapeutic agent, plays a pivotal role in the treatment of various cancers, including ovarian cancer, small cell lung cancer, and certain types of leukemia [1]. As a semisynthetic derivative of camptothecin, it falls under the category of topoisomerase I inhibitors, which are crucial in cancer therapy due to their targeted mechanism of action and effectiveness in managing malignancies [2]. TOP is primarily employed in the treatment of cancers that have proven to be resistant to conventional therapies [3]. The drug exerts its anti-cancer effects by inhibiting topoisomerase I, an enzyme crucial for DNA replication and transcription. TOP exhibits a pharmacokinetic profile that necessitates careful consideration in clinical applications. It is well-absorbed following intravenous administration, with its bioavailability significantly influenced by its binding to plasma proteins and cellular uptake mechanisms [4]. TOP is predominantly metabolized in the liver and eliminated through renal excretion, highlighting the importance of considering patient-specific factors such as hepatic and renal function when administering the drug [1]. The therapeutic use of TOP is often accompanied by a spectrum of side effects and toxicities, common to many chemotherapeutic agents [4]. These include myelosuppression, leading to neutropenia, anemia, and thrombocytopenia, as well as gastrointestinal disturbances such as nausea and vomiting [5]. Other side effects may include alopecia, fatigue, and increased risk of infections, necessitating comprehensive monitoring and supportive care to mitigate these adverse effects. The determination of TOP levels in blood plasma is important for optimizing therapeutic efficacy and minimizing toxicity [2]. Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) enables the personalization of dosing regimens based on the pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties of TOP, considering the individual patient's metabolism and excretion capabilities. Accurate measurement of plasma concentrations can aid in adjusting doses to achieve optimal therapeutic levels, thereby enhancing the drug's efficacy while reducing the risk of adverse effects.

Cobalt (Co2+) is a transition metal element that plays critical roles in various biological and industrial processes. As a component of vitamin B12, cobalt is essential for nervous system function and red blood cell formation [6]. The cobalt ion forms the central coordinating atom in B12 coenzymes. Cobalt deficiency can lead to anemia and neurological disorders. At higher doses, however, cobalt can elicit toxicity affecting the lungs, heart, and brain [7]. Excessive accumulation leads to polycythemia, cardiomyopathy, and impaired cognitive function [8]. Major natural sources of cobalt in water include weathering of rocks, volcanic emissions, and atmospheric deposition. Anthropogenic inputs arise from mining activities, fossil fuel combustion, waste incineration, and industrial discharges [9]. Typical cobalt concentrations in unpolluted surface and groundwaters are 0.1–10 μg L-1, while polluted waters can reach up to 1000 μg L-1 [10]. Monitoring cobalt levels in water is important because elevated concentrations above 300 μg L-1 can potentially cause adverse health effects in humans if there is prolonged consumption [11]. Cobalt is also a micronutrient for phytoplankton growth, so its levels influence aquatic ecosystems. Furthermore, cobalt is an indicator of overall water quality and anthropogenic inputs [8]. Therefore, regular monitoring of cobalt in surface and groundwaters is essential to identify pollution sources, control water quality, and minimize risks of chronic toxicity to human and ecological receptors due to cobalt or co-occurring contaminants. A range of analytical techniques, including voltametric [12–15], chromatographic [16–18], and spectroscopic methods [19–22], were utilized for the determination of cobalt and TOP.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials that have emerged as versatile applications across a broad range of fields [23–26]. CDs are small carbon-based nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in size, that exhibit unique optoelectronic properties [27,28]. The fluorescence emitted by CDs is tunable, stable, and dependent on the particle size and surface chemistry [29,30]. CDs possess advantages of low toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and photostability [31,32]. CDs can be produced through various methods including electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, acidic oxidation, ultrasonication, microwave synthesis, and hydrothermal treatment [33–35]. The synthesis route affects the final structure, surface state, and emissive behavior of the CDs. Major applications of CDs include sensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, optoelectronics, drug delivery, and theranostics [36]. Their high photoluminescence quantum yields, tunable emissions, excellent biocompatibility, and surface functionalization capacity make CDs ideal for fluorescence-based bioanalysis and biosensing [37].

Doping carbon dots with heteroatoms like sulphur or nitrogen can significantly enhance their properties and expand their applications [38]. Sulphur and nitrogen contain lone pairs that can modify the carbon dot surface chemistry. This allows tuning of the band gap to achieve desirable optical and electronic properties [39]. S, N-doping introduces new emissive states below the conduction band minimum, enabling controlled fluorescence across the visible spectrum. In addition, S/N-doped carbon dots exhibit enhanced photostability and quantum yields compared to undoped counterparts [40]. The presence of sulphur and nitrogen increases surface defects which improve carbon dot performance for sensing and catalysis applications through interactions with analytes [40]. Sulphanilic acid and phthalic acid were employed in the hydrothermal synthesis process to produce nitrogen and sulphur doped red emissive carbon dots (R-NS@CDs).

The primary objective of our study is to develop a method for the accurate sensing of both TOP and cobalt ions (Co2+). A dual probe composed of R-NS@CDs (red emission) and complexed TOP (green emission) is employed for the ratiometric detection of Co2+. The fluorescence intensity of R-NS@CDs is enhanced in the presence of Co2+, while the signal from complexed TOP remains unaffected and serves as a reliable reference signal during the detection process. To prevent interference from free TOP molecules competing with R-NS@CDs for Co2+ binding, which could affect accurate Co2+ concentration determination, TOP must be added in its pre-complexed form with Co2+ rather than its free, uncomplexed state. The developed ratiometric fluorescence method enabled selective determination of Co2+ in environmental water samples.

In a similar ratiometric approach, the determination of TOP is also achieved within a system consisting of R-NS@CDs and Co2+. Upon the addition of TOP, it forms a complex with Co2+, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system. Simultaneously, the fluorescence of TOP increases, allowing for the ratiometric quantification of TOP based on the changes in fluorescence signals. The developed ratiometric fluorescence method enabled sensitive quantification of TOP in complex rabbit plasma matrices for pharmacokinetic analysis.

This work explores the potential for simultaneous and independent qualification of two different analytes within a single system, offering a novel approach to tackle this analytical challenge. Furthermore, the ratiometric design ingeniously utilizes TOP and Co2+ as both analytes and fluorescence modulators. This dual functionality allows for expedited and low-cost profiling of multiple targets, with applications in environmental monitoring and clinical analysis. The sensor design also benefits from simplicity, selectivity, and applicability to complex real samples. The use of reference signal eliminates common causes of fluorescence intensity fluctuations, ensuring signal stability and improving quantification accuracy.

Sulphanilic acid, phthalic acid and Cobalt (II) chloride (98.5 %) were purchased from Merck, Darmstadt, Germany. Sodium hydroxide, acetic acid, phosphoric acid, boric acid, and hydrochloric acid were purchased from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. Zinc chloride, nickel chloride, potassium chloride, ferric chloride, magnesium sulphate, copper sulphate, lead nitrate, sodium nitrate, and sodium chlorate were provided by Alpha Chemia (Mumbai, India). TOP (99.0 %) was obtained from Hikma Pharmaceutical Factory, Cairo, Egypt. Hycamtin® vial containing 4.0 mg TOP per vial was purchased from a local drug store. Throughout the study, double distilled water (DDW) and analytical grade reagents were utilized.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a JEM-1400 Flash microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffractive (XRD) pattern was obtained using PW 1700 diffractometer (Eindhoven, Netherlands) with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were collected on a UV-1601 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) with slit width of 5 nm. Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was used to record the data for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The mean diameter and zeta potential of the synthesized particles were analyzed using a laser-scattering technique (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK).

The R-NS@CDs probe was synthesized through a hydrothermal method using sulphanilic acid and phthalic acid as precursors. First, 1 g of sulphanilic acid and 0.5 g of phthalic acid were dissolved in 50 mL of DDW. This solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 6.0 h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the resulting solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter followed by dialysis in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to remove unreacted reagents and impurities. Finally, the dialyzed dispersion was lyophilized to obtain a powdered product which was stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield of the prepared R-NS@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum yield calculations can be found in the Supplementary Data file.

For determination of Co2+, 1.0 mL of R-NS@CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) and 1.0 mL complexed TOP (500.0 ng mL−1 TOP: 1000 ng mL−1 Co2+) were mixed with 1.0 mL of varying concentrations of Co2+ in 10.0 mL flasks and diluting with B.R. buffer (pH 70.) in a 10.0 mL volumetric flask. The final concentration of complexed TOP (50.0 ng mL−1 TOP: 100 ng mL−1 Co2+). The final measured concentration range for Co2+ was 1.0 – 90.0 ng mL−1. After mixing all the components in the volumetric flask, the solution was allowed to react for 5.0 min to ensure a complete reaction. A blank solution was prepared in a similar manner, but instead of adding 1.0 mL of Co2+, 1.0 mL of DDW was added. Emission spectra of these solutions were measured using 300 nm excitation and recording the fluorescence intensities at 545 nm and 680 nm.

To determine TOP, 1.0 mL of R-NS@CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) and 1.0 mL of Co2+ (900 ng mL−1) were mixed in 10.0 mL flasks. The final concentration of Co2+ is 90.0 ng mL−1. The solution was allowed to react for 5.0 min. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of varying TOP concentrations was added to the reaction mixture. The solutions were then brought to volume with B.R. buffer (pH 7.0) in 10.0 mL volumetric flasks and incubated for 1.0 min. The final measured concentration range for TOP was 5.0 – 160.0 ng mL−1. A blank solution was prepared in a similar manner, but instead of adding 1.0 mL of TOP, 1.0 mL of DDW was added. Emission spectra were measured using 300 nm excitation and recording the fluorescence intensities at 545 nm and 680 nm.

To prepare spiked rabbit plasma samples, different concentrations of TOP were added to drug-free plasma. For each spiked sample, 200 µL of plasma were mixed with 800 µL of cold methanol to precipitate the plasma proteins. This mixture was vortexed for 90 s, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. After centrifugation, the methanolic supernatant layer was collected carefully. The supernatant aliquots were stored at −20 °C until ready for subsequent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TOP in real plasma samples (Pharmacokinetic study)
ce:section.ce:para: The pharmacokinetic study was conducted in full compliance with ethical regulations, under a protocol approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at Assiut University. The experiment adhered to the guiding principles in the Helsinki Declaration for humane treatment of animals. The study utilized six healthy New Zealand white rabbits acquired from a reputable licensed vendor. The rabbits weighed approximately 2.0 kg each, with weights ranging from 1.8 to 2.2 kg across the group. Prior to beginning the experiment, the rabbits underwent health assessments by a veterinarian to confirm their well-being. The rabbits were individually housed in cages providing adequate space for movement. To acclimate the animals, a 15-day adjustment period was provided, during which the rabbits became accustomed to the housing environment and handling procedures. Throughout the acclimatization and experiment, the rabbits had free access to a standard commercial diet and water except during a 12-hour fasting period directly before the experiment commenced. The housing room was maintained at a temperature of 25 ± 2 °C and a humidity of 55 ± 5 %. To assess the pharmacokinetics of TOP, six rabbits received intravenous infusion at a dose of 0.1 mg kg−1. Blood samples (1.0 mL each) were collected from the marginal ear vein at several timepoints after dosing: 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 12 h. The blood samples were transferred to tubes containing heparin anticoagulant. The tubes were centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 15 min to separate the plasma. The isolated plasma was then stored at −20 °C in light-protected containers to prevent sample degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Co2+ in environmental waters
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze Co2+ content in environmental water samples collected from Ibrahimiya Canal in Assiut Province, Egypt. Environmental water samples collected are processed following a previously established method [41]. Environmental water samples are collected and stored in polypropylene bottles, each containing 1.0 ml of concentrated nitric acid to preserve the sample integrity. For analysis, a portion of the water sample is filtered using Whatman No. 40 filter paper to remove particulate matter. Directly after filtration, the filtered water sample is treated with a mixture of 0.5 ml concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and 5 ml of concentrated nitric acid (HNO3) in a fume cupboard. After cooling, the acid-digested sample is neutralized with a dilute solution of ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) to bring the pH to a neutral range. During the neutralization process, 1–2 ml of a 0.01 % (w/v) tartrate solution is added. The neutralized solution is then filtered to remove any insoluble residues. The clear filtrate is transferred into a 10-mL calibrated flask and made up to the mark with de-ionized water, preparing it for the analysis of Co2+ content.

The sensor design utilizes R-NS@CDs whose fluorescence is modulated by Co2+ and TOP in distinct ways. For Co2+ detection, the fluorescence of R-NS@CDs at 680 nm is enhanced by Co2+, while the emission of pre-complexed TOP with Co2+ at 545 nm remains constant, allowing ratiometric quantification of Co2 + . Critically, TOP is added in its pre-complexed Co2+-bound form to avoid interference from free TOP molecules competing with R-NS@CDs for Co2+ binding. For TOP detection, the roles are reversed − TOP displaces Co2+ from the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ complex, quenching the 680 nm fluorescence but simultaneously enhancing its own emission at 545 nm. This ratiometric change between the two emission channels enables sensitive detection of TOP concentrations.

The morphologies and size distribution of R-NS@CDs were obtained by the TEM. As revealed in Fig. 1
A, the prepared R-NS@CDs generally exhibited uniform spherical nanostructures with good dispersibility, and the diameters mostly fell within the range of 2.0–5.0 nm with the mean particle size of approximately 3.0 nm (Fig. 1 A inset). The pattern of XRD showed a peak at 19.0 in 2θ diffraction which corresponded to the (002) plane of carbon which indicates disordered carbon atoms (Fig. 1 B).

The FT-IR spectrum verifies successful R-NS@CDs synthesis as shown in Fig. 1 C. The broad peak at 3413 cm−1 indicates O-H stretching and the shoulder peak at 3260 cm−1 shows N-H stretching. The peaks at 1545 cm−1, 1212 cm−1 and 1133 cm−1 correspond to bending and stretching vibrations of aromatic amines, confirming incorporation of amine groups [26,27]. Aromatic C-H bending at 935 cm−1 and C-S stretching at 650 cm−1 suggest presence of aryl and sulphonic acid moieties respectively [28,30]. A very small peak at 1732 cm−1 likely indicates low remaining carbonyl groups from the precursors. The lack of distinct CO peaks suggests conversion during synthesis or presence in low concentrations compared to the amine, aromatic and sulphonic acid functional groups.

The surface composition and functional groups of the synthesized R-NS@CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey scan displayed four peaks at 169, 286, 399, and 532 eV binding energies, representing S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s elements, respectively (Fig. 2
A). High-resolution C1s spectrum exhibited four main peaks; a peak at 284.5 eV binding energy attributed to C-C bonds of the graphitic structure, indicating abundance of graphite structures in R-NS@CDs. A peak at 285.2 eV was assigned to C-O, C-N, and C-S bonds. Peaks at 287.4 eV and 288.3 eV corresponded to CO and OCO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2 B). The O1s high resolution spectrum showed three peaks at 532.3, 533.5, and 534.5 eV binding energies indicating COH/COC /SO3, OCO, and COOH functional groups, respectively (Fig. 2 C). The N1s spectrum displayed three peaks at 401.3, 399.5 and 398.9 eV, representing N-H, N-C and N = C bonds (Fig. 2 D). Finally, the S2p high resolution spectrum exhibited two peaks at 169.4 and 170.1 eV binding energies, corresponding to the sulphonate groups (S2p1/2 and S2p3/2) (Fig. 2 E).

The absorption properties of the synthesized R-NS@CDs were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 3
A. A distinct peak at 246 nm was observed, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 carbon. Additionally, a shoulder peak around 285 nm was observed, indicating the presence of n-π* transitions. Furthermore, a broad and weak peak around 500 nm was observed, which may be attributed to the presence of a highly conjugated system during the formation of carbon dots. The fluorescence emission spectrum in Fig. 3 A displayed a peak emission of the R-NS@CDs at 680 nm when excited at 300 nm, which was chosen over 600 nm excitation wavelength due to higher emission intensity. The R-NS@CDs exhibited excitation-independent fluorescence behavior, meaning the emission wavelength was constant while varying excitation wavelength, as shown in Fig. 3 B. The emission wavelength of R-NS@CDs remained constant when excited within the range of 260 to 320 nm. However, an increase in excitation wavelength from 260 to 300 nm resulted in a noticeable intensity enhancement, but further increasing the excitation wavelength led to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Based on the previous observation, an excitation wavelength of 300 nm was selected for further experiments. Fluorescence quantum yield of the R-NS@CDs was calculated to be 13.1 % using quinine sulphate as a standard.

The fluorescence emission of the R-NS@CDs was studied under various conditions to evaluate its stability under different conditions. The R-NS@CDs showed stable fluorescence from pH 5.0 to 10.0 with minimal variation. However, at lower pH between 2.0–4.0, the fluorescence was quenched, likely due to protonation effects on the surface groups of the R-NS@CDs [42], as seen in Fig. S1 A. The fluorescence maintained consistent intensity across a wide NaCl concentration range from 0.01–3.0 mol/L, demonstrating the probe's stability under different ionic strengths and suitability for physiological conditions ( Fig. S1 B). Additionally, the R-NS@CDs fluorescence had negligible variation under UV irradiation for 5.0 h, exhibiting excellent photostability as shown in Fig. S1 C.

The key parameters for the dual detection of Co2+ and TOP using R-NS@CDs were systematically optimized to achieve maximum detection sensitivity. The pH range of 4.0 to 11.0 was investigated, and it was found that the optimal pH for Co2+ complexation with R-NS@CDs and the subsequent enhancement effect on fluorescence intensity (ΔF) of R-NS@CDs was pH 7. Higher pH levels (above pH 7.0) were observed to reduce Co2+ complexation to R-NS@CDs and diminish the enhancement effect on fluorescence intensity, as depicted in Fig. S2 A. This reduction at higher pH can be attributed to precipitation of Co2+ as hydroxide that prevents complexation with R-NS@CDs. The interaction time between Co2+ and R-NS@CDs was optimized at 5.0 min, allowing sufficient time for binding and consequently highest fluorescence enhancement (ΔF) ( Fig. S2 B). To detect TOP, a range of Co2+ concentrations from 10.0 to 2000.0 ng mL−1 were examined. Among these concentrations, an optimal concentration of 160.0 ng mL−1 was selected, as it resulted in maximum fluorescence enhancement (ΔF) ( Fig. S2 C).

To ensure TOP serves as an effective reference signal during Co2+ determination, it must be in a complexed form with Co2+ rather than its free form. Therefore, optimization of the Co2+: TOP ratio for forming the complexed TOP was carried out. The R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system exhibiting maximum fluorescence enhancement was used as the starting point. Different concentrations of Co2+ were sequentially added (5.0–300.0 ng mL−1) to a fixed TOP concentration (50.0 ng mL−1) to determine the optimal ratio where no further quenching of the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ fluorescence occurs, indicating complete complexation of TOP. 100.0 ng mL−1 of Co2+ was found to be the optimal concentration for achieving complete complexation with TOP, beyond which no additional quenching was observed ( Fig. S2 D). The presence of free TOP in the system can interfere with the accurate determination of Co2+ concentration interacting with R-NS@CDs, as free TOP molecules may compete with R-NS@CDs for binding to Co2+, thereby affecting the observed fluorescence modulation.

For TOP detection, incubation times of 0.5 to 10.0 min were examined, with 1.0 min being optimal for observing analyte-induced quenching of R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system ( Fig. S2 E). Excitation wavelength scans revealed dual emission peaks at 680 nm for R-NS@CDs and 545 nm for TOP after excitation at 300 nm, enabling simultaneous detection. It is crucial to note that 300 nm is the only wavelength that provides a balanced dual signal, allowing for the simultaneous detection of both R-NS@CDs and TOP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances for Co2+ and TOP
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence detection of Co2+ was performed using the dual-emission system of R-NS@CDs (680 nm) and complexed TOP (545 nm). Upon addition of increasing concentrations of Co2+, the fluorescence intensity at 680 nm showed a proportional enhancement. In contrast, the complexed TOP emission peak at 545 nm remained unchanged by the addition of Co2+. This allowed ratiometric detection based on the fluorescence intensity ratio F680/F545. As shown in Fig. 4
A, this ratiometric intensity exhibited a continuous increase as the Co2+ concentration was raised from 5.0 to 160.0 ng mL−1. The calibration curve constructed by plotting F680/F545 versus Co2+ concentration demonstrated good linearity over this range (R2 = 0.9944), as seen in Fig. 4 B. The fitted linear equations were F680/F545 = 0.0034C + 1.46. The limit of detection (LOD) for the assay was determined to be 1.51 ng mL−1 using the 3σ/S method, where σ represents the standard deviation of the intercept and S represents the slope of the linear calibration plot. The consistent fluorescence of TOP at 545 nm could serve as an internal standard, while the R-NS@CDs emission at 680 nm responded sensitively and selectively to Co2+.

The fluorescence detection system for TOP utilizes a combination of Co2+ and R-NS@CDs. When TOP is added to this system, the fluorescence signal at 680 nm emitted by the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ complex is quenched. Simultaneously, the native fluorescence of TOP at 545 nm increases ( Fig. 4 C). Fig. 4 D shows the linear relationship between TOP concentration (1.0–90.0 ng mL−1) and the change in fluorescence intensity ratio F545/F680. A strong correlation (R2 = 0.9965) was observed between the F545/F680 ratio and TOP concentration. The linear regression equation was determined to be: F545/F680 = 0.0072C + 0.080, Where C is the TOP concentration. Based on this equation, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.37 ng mL−1 for TOP. The error bars represented the standard deviation calculated from the average fluorescence intensity of three parallel samples. The obtained RSD ranged from 0.43 % to 1.91 %.

The proposed method offers significant advantages for the detection of both Co2+ and TOP compared to previously reported techniques. As shown in Table S1 , the method achieves a remarkably low detection limit for Co2+, surpassing most other methods in its sensitivity. Notably, this approach is one of the few capable of determining TOP concentrations, and it demonstrates higher sensitivity than the only available fluorometric method for TOP detection. The ratiometric approach employed in this method provides an additional reference signal, enhancing its reliability and accuracy. Unlike some existing methods that may suffer from interference issues in biological matrices, the proposed technique exhibits excellent selectivity, minimizing potential interferences. Furthermore, this method is rapid, cost-effective, and requires minimal sample preparation, making it a practical and economical choice compared to other methods that may be more complex, time-consuming, or resource-intensive.

To assess the reliability of the newly proposed probe, a series of experiments were conducted, including intra-day and inter-day precisions, as well as stability studies. Three different concentrations of Co2+ and TOP were utilized for these evaluations. Specifically, Co2+ concentrations of 20.0, 40.0, and 60.0 ng mL−1, and TOP concentrations of 5.0, 10.0, and 20.0 ng mL−1 were employed. The results, expressed as recovery ± RSD, were analyzed. As shown in Table S2 , all RSD values were found to be below 2.45 %. These findings indicate that the probe exhibited excellent precision in the conducted experiments.

While the developed ratiometric fluorescent system demonstrated high sensitivity for Co2+ detection, selectivity was crucial for application to complex environmental water samples. To improve selectivity, tartrate was added during sample treatment to mask interfering cations that form strong complex with tartrate. Tartrate exhibits higher stability constants when complexed with metal ions other than Co2+, forming relatively weak complexes with Co2+ compared to its interactions with other metal species [43,44]. Selectivity experiments were performed by adding different metal ions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Ni2+) individually into the probe solution at 500.0 ng mL−1 concentration. As shown in Fig. S3 A, only Co2+ caused significant enhancement of R-NS@CDs fluorescence, while other ions had negligible effects on fluorescence. Additionally, common anions like Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2-, and ClO3 - had insignificant impacts on the fluorescence response, indicating a stronger selective interaction of the probe with Co2+ compared to interferences.

The selectivity of R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system for TOP detection was evaluated by testing potential interfering substances. These included metal ions, biomolecules, and commonly co-administered drugs. As shown in Fig. S3 B, most interferents had a minor effect on the F545/F680 ratio compared to TOP. Specifically, various cations (Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+), biomolecules (ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), dopamine, bovine serum albumin (BSA)), and drugs (cisplatin, imatinib, irinotecan, doxorubicin (DOX)) were added to R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system at 500 ng mL−1 along with 5.0 ng mL−1 TOP. Only TOP caused a significant increase in the F545/F680 ratio compared to the blank and other potential interferants. TOP specifically and substantially increased the F545/F680 ratio, while common interferents had minimal effects. This demonstrates the selectivity of the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ probe for sensitive and specific TOP detection even in complex samples. The ratiometric measurement offered reliable TOP quantitation by minimizing interferences from sample matrix effects.

The synthesized fluorescent R-NS@CDs displayed an emission peak at 680 nm. Upon addition of Co2+, the fluorescence intensity at 680 nm was amplified due to the Chelation-Enhanced Fluorescence (CHEF) effect. This was evidenced by the increase and slight red-shift (6.0 nm) of the 680 nm peak after adding Co2+, as shown in Fig. 5
A. This suggests chemical interaction between the R-NS@CDs and Co2+. FTIR spectroscopy ( Fig. 5 B) provided further evidence for coordination between Co2+ and the functional groups on the R-NS@CDs surface. The broad –OH and –NH peaks at 3413 cm−1 and 3260 cm−1 shifting to a narrower peak at 3438 cm−1 indicates coordination of the hydroxyl and amine groups with Co2+. This involves binding of Co2+ to the O and N donor groups. The amine peak at 1545 cm−1 becoming less intense and shifting slightly to 1530 cm−1 suggests coordination of the amines with Co2+. This is due to binding of Co2+ with the nitrogen on the amines. The forked aromatic amine peaks at 1212 cm−1 and 1133 cm−1 transitioning to a broad peak at 1070 cm−1 indicates perturbation of the C-N bonding pattern upon complexation with Co2+. The aromatic C-H bending peak at 935 cm−1 shifting to 850 cm−1 and becoming less intense reveals interaction of Co2+ with the aromatic rings. The Co2+ likely coordinates with the pi electrons. The sulphonic acid C-S stretching peak at 650 cm−1 shifting down to 542 cm−1 and reducing in intensity suggests participation of the sulphonate group in binding to Co2+. The FTIR peak changes after adding Co2+ are consistent with coordination of the metal ion to the hydroxyl, amine, aromatic and sulphonic acid moieties on the carbon dot surface. This involves donating of the O, N and S lone pairs and aromatic pi electrons to bond with the Co2+. Furthermore, upon the addition of Co2+, the zeta potential of the R-NS@CDs exhibited an increase from −25.2 mV to −20.5 mV. This observation suggests that the R-NS@CDs have the capability to chelate with Co2+ ions as depicted in Fig. 5 C.

For TOP detection, the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ combined system was utilized as a sensing platform. TOP forms a complex with Co2+ ( Fig. 5 A), liberating Co2+ from its coordination with the carbon dots. This caused quenching of the R-NS@CDs 680 nm emission peak and the native TOP peak at 545 nm is increased. The fluorescence intensity of the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system decreased progressively with increasing concentrations of TOP ( Fig. 4 C). This quantitative quenching effect confirms that TOP has a stronger interaction with Co2+ compared to the carbon dots. Control experiments verified that TOP itself does not affect the fluorescence of R-NS@CDs in the absence of Co2+. Therefore, the quenching can be attributed specifically to the release of Co2+ from their coordination complex with the R-NS@CDs as they bind preferentially with TOP. Furthermore, the quantum yield of R-NS@CDs was determined both before and after the addition of Co2+, resulting in values of 13.1 % and 15.2 %, respectively.

For the binding interaction of Co2+ with TOP, the binding constants (K) and binding sites (n) were determined by the following equation:
l o g
F 0
- F
F
= l o g
K
+ n l o g
Q
F 0 is the emission intensity without a quencher, F is the emission intensity after the addition of a quencher, K is the corresponding binding constant, Q is the concentration of the quencher (TOP), and n is the binding sites number. Where, K is the binding constant, that can be determined from the intercept of log
l o g
F 0
- F
F
versus log[Q] as illustrated in Fig. 5 D. Thus, the following value for binding constant; K = 3.98 × 103 M−1 and number of binding sites (n) = 0.78 were obtained. The value of “n” approximately equals to 1 indicates the existence of one binding site in TOP for Co2+. To further investigate the mechanism of interaction between R-NS@CDs and Co2+, TEM images were taken before and after addition of TOP to this system. The increase in R-NS@CDs particle size after addition of Co2+ (Fig. 5 E) indicates cobalt-induced aggregation and cross-linking of the dots. This interparticle aggregation due to Co2+ complexation leads to a red-shift in fluorescence to longer wavelengths, consistent with the reduced quantum confinement effect in larger particles [45]. The subsequent decrease in particle size upon TOP addition to the R-NS@CDs-Co2+ system (Fig. 5 F) can be attributed to withdrawal of Co2+ from the R-NS@CDs surface due to preferential binding with TOP. This disrupts the interparticle cross-linking by Co2+, breaking apart the aggregates and restoring the individual small dot sizes. The fluorescence blue-shifts back to shorter wavelengths as the particle size decreases, validating the relationship between quantum confinement and fluorescence emission energy. Thus, the observed fluorescence enhancement may be attributed to Chelation-Enhanced Fluorescence, as well as the cobalt-induced aggregation and cross-linking of the dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Co2+ in real water samples
Application to real plasma and pharmacokinetic study of TOP
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the ratiometric fluorescence technique for analysis of TOP in complex biological samples was demonstrated through spiked rabbit plasma studies. TOP was spiked into plasma at concentrations typically observed clinically after intravenous administration (10.0–50.0 ng mL−1) [4]. The method successfully detected TOP levels ranging from 1.0-90.0 ng mL−1 in the spiked plasma, as shown in Fig. S4 A. Linear regression analysis of the measured TOP concentrations in spiked plasma produced a calibration equation (F545/F680 = 0.0076C + 0.047; R2 = 0.9907) that can reliably quantify TOP content. This confirms the applicability of this approach for therapeutic drug monitoring by accurately determining TOP levels in plasma. The method also exhibited a low detection limit of 0.39 ng mL−1 for TOP in plasma. The high sensitivity enables reliable quantification of TOP at clinically relevant levels.

The pharmacokinetics of TOP were investigated in a group of six healthy New Zealand rabbits. A single oral dose of 0.1 mg kg−1 was administered, and the resulting pharmacokinetic profile of TOP is illustrated in Fig. S4 B. This profile provides insights into the absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination of TOP in the rabbits' bodies over time. The maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) of 48.44 ng mL−1 indicates rapid achievement of high TOP levels following IV dosing. The initial half-life (t1/2α) of 0.012 h shows rapid distribution from the central/plasma compartment. The terminal half-life (t1/2β) of 6.65 h reflects the elimination rate and suggests a moderate clearance rate. The small volume of distribution (Vc = 0.0002 L kg−1; Vp = 0.0025 L kg−1) indicates limited distribution of TOP from plasma to tissues. The elimination rate constant (kel) of 1.29 h−1 translates to the moderate half-life. The total clearance (CL) of 0.0003 L/kg/h appears relatively slow. The mean residence time (MRT) of 9.39 h is reasonably long and matches the t1/2. AUC0-12 of 245 ng.h mL−1 shows substantial exposure over 12 h. AUC0-∞ of 344 ng.h mL−1 indicates most of the exposure is within the first 12 h. The PK parameters demonstrate rapid IV uptake, moderate clearance, limited distribution, and reasonably sustained exposure of TOP, which is desirable for this chemotherapy drug.

In summary, this work successfully developed a dual-probe ratiometric fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective detection of cobalt ions and topotecan based on modulation of red-emissive nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dots. The strategy takes advantage of the analyte-induced fluorescence enhancement of R-NS@CDs by cobalt ions, alongside the quenching effect on the R-NS@CDs-cobalt complex fluorescence imparted by topotecan. Ratiometric detection in two modes enabled quantification of cobalt and topotecan with excellent selectivity and low detection limits. Mechanistic insights into the fluorescence modulation mechanisms were gained through spectroscopic and physicochemical characterization. The practical applicability for complex sample analysis was demonstrated through detection of cobalt in environmental samples and topotecan in plasma. The built-in calibration afforded by dual analyte-induced modulation of a single fluorescent probe makes this a simple yet powerful analytical approach. In the future, the sensitivity and selectivity of this ratiometric fluorescence technique can be further enhanced by optimizing the R-NS@CDs composition and rational design of the probe system. This work provides a promising dual-probe strategy for pharmaceutical and environmental analysis through facile mix-and-detect fluorescence detection.
This work was supported and funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) (grant number IMSIU-RG23083).

Ali M. Alaseem: Visualization, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Khalid Alhazzani: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Ahmed Z. Alanazi: Visualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Saud M. Alsanad: Validation, Software, Resources, Data curation. Osama A. Alkhamees: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Project administration, Data curation. Glowi Alasiri: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their gratitude to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) for their generous support and funding. The grant provided (grant number IMSIU-RG23083) has been instrumental in the successful completion of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110645.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
